([reporting mark](/wiki/Reporting_mark "Reporting mark") **LI**), or **LIRR**, is a [railroad](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") in the southeastern part of the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), stretching from [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") to the eastern tip of [Suffolk County](/wiki/Suffolk_County,_New_York "Suffolk County, New York") on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"). The railroad currently operates a public [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") service, with its freight operations contracted to the [New York and Atlantic Railway](/wiki/New_York_and_Atlantic_Railway "New York and Atlantic Railway"). With an average weekday ridership of 354,800 passengers in 2016, it is the [busiest commuter railroad](/wiki/List_of_United_States_commuter_rail_systems_by_ridership "List of United States commuter rail systems by ridership") in North America.[[5]](#cite_note-APTA-2016-Q4-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) It is also one of the world's few commuter systems that run 24/7 year-round.[[7]](#cite_note-7) It is [publicly owned](/wiki/Government-owned_corporation "Government-owned corporation") by the [Metropolitan Transportation Authority](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Metropolitan Transportation Authority"), which refers to it as **MTA Long Island Rail Road**. In 2025, the system had a ridership of 103,474,900, or about 336,300 per weekday as of the fourth quarter of 2025.

was chartered in 1834 to provide a daily service between [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") via a ferry connection between its [Greenport, New York](/wiki/Greenport,_Suffolk_County,_New_York "Greenport, Suffolk County, New York"), terminal on Long Island's North Fork and [Stonington, Connecticut](/wiki/Stonington,_Connecticut "Stonington, Connecticut"). This service was superseded in 1849 by the land route through Connecticut that became part of the [New York, New Haven and Hartford Railroad](/wiki/New_York,_New_Haven_and_Hartford_Railroad "New York, New Haven and Hartford Railroad"). The LIRR refocused its attentions towards serving Long Island, in competition with other railroads on the island. In the 1870s, railroad president [Conrad Poppenhusen](/wiki/Conrad_Poppenhusen "Conrad Poppenhusen") and his successor, [Austin Corbin](/wiki/Austin_Corbin "Austin Corbin") acquired all the railroads and consolidated them into the LIRR.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The **Long Island Rail Road** ([reporting mark](/wiki/Reporting_mark "Reporting mark") **LI**), or **LIRR**, is a [railroad](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") in the southeastern part of the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), stretching from [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") to the eastern tip of [Suffolk County](/wiki/Suffolk_County,_New_York "Suffolk County, New York") on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"). The railroad currently operates a public [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") service, with its freight operations contracted to the [New York and Atlantic Railway](/wiki/New_York_and_Atlantic_Railway "New York and Atlantic Railway"). With an average weekday ridership of 354,800 passengers in 2016, it is the [busiest commuter railroad](/wiki/List_of_United_States_commuter_rail_systems_by_ridership "List of United States commuter rail systems by ridership") in North America.[[5]](#cite_note-APTA-2016-Q4-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) It is also one of the world's few commuter systems that run 24/7 year-round.[[7]](#cite_note-7) It is [publicly owned](/wiki/Government-owned_corporation "Government-owned corporation") by the [Metropolitan Transportation Authority](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Metropolitan Transportation Authority"), which refers to it as **MTA Long Island Rail Road**. In 2025, the system had a ridership of 103,474,900, or about 336,300 per weekday as of the fourth quarter of 2025.

The LIRR logo combines the circular MTA logo with the text *Long Island Rail Road*, and appears on the sides of trains. The LIRR is one of two commuter rail systems owned by the MTA, the other being the [Metro-North Railroad](/wiki/Metro-North_Railroad "Metro-North Railroad") in the northern suburbs of the New York area. Established in 1834 (the first section between the Brooklyn waterfront and Jamaica opened on April 18, 1836) and having operated continuously since then, it is the oldest railroad in the United States still operating under its original name and charter.[[8]](#cite_note-LIRR175-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-abouttheLIRR-10) The railroad has been headquartered in the headhouse of [Jamaica Station](/wiki/Jamaica_Station "Jamaica Station"), the second busiest station in the system, since 1913.[[11]](#cite_note-hq-11)

There are 126 stations and more than 700 miles (1,100 km) of track[[10]](#cite_note-abouttheLIRR-10) on its two main lines running the full length of the island and eight major branches, with the passenger railroad system totaling 319 [route](/wiki/Network_length_(transport)#Route_length "Network length (transport)") miles (513 km).[[12]](#cite_note-mtabudget-12) As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit), the LIRR's budget for expenditures was $1.6 billion plus $450 million for debt service, which it supports through the collection of fares (which cover 43% of total expenses) along with dedicated taxes and other MTA revenue.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Long_Island_Railroad_100th_Anniversary_cover_1934.jpg/250px-Long_Island_Railroad_100th_Anniversary_cover_1934.jpg)](/wiki/File:Long_Island_Railroad_100th_Anniversary_cover_1934.jpg)

LIRR (Montauk & NY) [RPO](/wiki/Railway_Post_Office "Railway Post Office") cover (TR27) for the railroad's 100th anniversary in April 1934

The **Long Island Rail Road Company** was chartered in 1834 to provide a daily service between [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") via a ferry connection between its [Greenport, New York](/wiki/Greenport,_Suffolk_County,_New_York "Greenport, Suffolk County, New York"), terminal on Long Island's North Fork and [Stonington, Connecticut](/wiki/Stonington,_Connecticut "Stonington, Connecticut"). This service was superseded in 1849 by the land route through Connecticut that became part of the [New York, New Haven and Hartford Railroad](/wiki/New_York,_New_Haven_and_Hartford_Railroad "New York, New Haven and Hartford Railroad"). The LIRR refocused its attentions towards serving Long Island, in competition with other railroads on the island. In the 1870s, railroad president [Conrad Poppenhusen](/wiki/Conrad_Poppenhusen "Conrad Poppenhusen") and his successor, [Austin Corbin](/wiki/Austin_Corbin "Austin Corbin") acquired all the railroads and consolidated them into the LIRR.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The LIRR was unprofitable for much of its history. In 1900, the [Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad "Pennsylvania Railroad") (PRR) bought a controlling interest as part of [its plan for direct access to Manhattan](/wiki/New_York_Tunnel_Extension "New York Tunnel Extension"), which began on September 8, 1910. The wealthy PRR subsidized the LIRR during the first half of the new century, allowing expansion and modernization.[[8]](#cite_note-LIRR175-8) Electric operation began in 1905.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the railroad industry's downturn and dwindling profits caused the PRR to stop subsidizing the LIRR, and the LIRR went into [receivership](/wiki/Receivership "Receivership") in 1949. The [State of New York](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_(state) "Government of New York (state)"), realizing how important the railroad was to Long Island's future, began to subsidize the railroad in the 1950s and continued doing so into the 1960s. In June 1965, the state finalized an agreement to buy the LIRR from the PRR for $65 million.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The LIRR was placed under the control of a new [Metropolitan Commuter Transit Authority](/wiki/Metropolitan_Commuter_Transit_Authority "Metropolitan Commuter Transit Authority").[[18]](#cite_note-18) The MCTA was rebranded the [Metropolitan Transportation Authority](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Metropolitan Transportation Authority") in 1968 when it incorporated several other New York City-area transit agencies.[[19]](#cite_note-QnsGazette-QnsPubTrans-51stBDay-2015-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) With MTA subsidies the LIRR modernized further, continuing to be the busiest commuter railroad in the United States.[[8]](#cite_note-LIRR175-8) The company was officially converted to a [subsidiary public benefit corporation](/wiki/New_York_state_public-benefit_corporations "New York state public-benefit corporations") in 1980.[[3]](#cite_note-subsidiary-3)

The LIRR is one of the few railroads that have survived as intact companies from their original charters to the present.[[8]](#cite_note-LIRR175-8)

### 21st century expansions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 21st century expansions")]

#### East Side Access

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: East Side Access")]

Main article: [East Side Access](/wiki/East_Side_Access "East Side Access")

The East Side Access project built a LIRR spur to Grand Central Terminal that will run in part via the lower level of the existing [63rd Street Tunnel](/wiki/63rd_Street_Tunnel "63rd Street Tunnel").[[21]](#cite_note-:3-21) The East Side Access project added a new eight-track terminal called [Grand Central Madison](/wiki/Grand_Central_Madison "Grand Central Madison") underneath the existing Grand Central Terminal.[[21]](#cite_note-:3-21) The project was first proposed in the 1968 [Program for Action](/wiki/Program_for_Action "Program for Action"), but due to various funding shortfalls, construction did not start until 2007.[[22]](#cite_note-22) As of April 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit), the project was expected to cost $11.1 billion and was tentatively scheduled to start service in December 2022.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Siff_2018-24)[[25]](#cite_note-Castillo_2018-25) It opened on January 25, 2023, with limited shuttle service between Jamaica and Grand Central.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) Full service to Grand Central began on February 27, 2023.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Several "readiness projects" were also completed to increase peak-hour capacity across the LIRR system in preparation for expanded peak-hour service after the completion of East Side Access.[[29]](#cite_note-TimesLedger_Readiness-29)[[30]](#cite_note-Jan_2013-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Nov_2017-31) The LIRR constructed a new platform for [Atlantic Terminal](/wiki/Atlantic_Terminal "Atlantic Terminal")-bound trains at [Jamaica station](/wiki/Jamaica_station "Jamaica station"),[[32]](#cite_note-32) converting most [Atlantic Branch](/wiki/Atlantic_Branch "Atlantic Branch") service between these two stations into a high-frequency shuttle.[[33]](#cite_note-MTA-LIRR-Hicksville-2016-33) The LIRR also installed a new storage track east of [Massapequa](/wiki/Massapequa_station "Massapequa station") and extended one east of [Great Neck station](/wiki/Great_Neck_station "Great Neck station"),[[31]](#cite_note-Nov_2017-31)[[34]](#cite_note-Great_Neck_Record-34) in addition to expanding the train yard at [Ronkonkoma](/wiki/Ronkonkoma_station "Ronkonkoma station").[[31]](#cite_note-Nov_2017-31)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-schedule-36) An expansion of the yard at [Port Washington](/wiki/Port_Washington_station "Port Washington station") was also proposed, but as of September 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit), the MTA has not come to an agreement with the [Town of North Hempstead](/wiki/Town_of_North_Hempstead "Town of North Hempstead"),[[37]](#cite_note-Nov_2020-37): 65 resulting in the project being postponed indefinitely.[[38]](#cite_note-senate092822-38)[[39]](#cite_note-Nov_2021-39): 61

There are also plans to build [a new station](/wiki/Sunnyside_station_(New_York) "Sunnyside station (New York)") in the Queens neighborhood of [Sunnyside](/wiki/Sunnyside,_Queens "Sunnyside, Queens"), in between the New York terminals and the [Woodside](/wiki/Woodside_station_(LIRR) "Woodside station (LIRR)") station, serving as a rail hub for all LIRR branches and potentially some Amtrak, Metro North (New Haven Line) and [New Jersey Transit](/wiki/NJ_Transit "NJ Transit") trains, as well.[[40]](#cite_note-:4-40) The Sunnyside station is to be built after the completion of East Side Access, due to current capacity constraints.[[40]](#cite_note-:4-40)

#### Main Line projects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Main Line projects")]

[![The completed second track, as viewed from the reconstructed Wyandanch station.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/WyandanchDoubleTracks2018RRCrossing.jpg/250px-WyandanchDoubleTracks2018RRCrossing.jpg)](/wiki/File:WyandanchDoubleTracks2018RRCrossing.jpg)

The Ronkonkoma Branch's completed second track at the Wyandanch station

In 2012, the LIRR started adding a second track along the formerly single-tracked section of the Main Line between [Farmingdale](/wiki/Farmingdale_station "Farmingdale station") and [Ronkonkoma stations](/wiki/Ronkonkoma_station "Ronkonkoma station") to increase track capacity and allow for enhanced service options.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The project was completed in September 2018.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

As part of the preparations for East Side Access's opening,[[44]](#cite_note-MTA_2007_Third_Track_Presentation-44) the LIRR also widened the two-track sections of the Main Line between [Floral Park](/wiki/Floral_Park_station "Floral Park station") and [Hicksville stations](/wiki/Hicksville_station "Hicksville station") to three tracks, in addition to eliminating each of the grade crossings and rebuilding all of the stations along this stretch of the Main Line.[[45]](#cite_note-NYT_Fitzsimmons_01.05.2016-45)[[46]](#cite_note-MTA_Third_Track_01.05.2016-46) Work on the third-track project started in September 2018.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) The project was completed in 2022, in time for the opening of East Side Access.[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)

The larger Belmont Park Redevelopment Project called for a new [Elmont station](/wiki/Elmont_station "Elmont station") between the [Queens Village](/wiki/Queens_Village_station "Queens Village station") and [Bellerose](/wiki/Bellerose_station "Bellerose station") stations on the Main Line, to better serve the new [UBS Arena](/wiki/UBS_Arena "UBS Arena") in the Nassau County neighborhood of [Elmont](/wiki/Elmont,_New_York "Elmont, New York").[[52]](#cite_note-52) It was the first new station built by the LIRR in nearly 50 years; the last new station added was the former [Southampton College](/wiki/Southampton_College_(LIRR_station) "Southampton College (LIRR station)") station on the Montauk Branch, which opened in 1976 and closed in 1998, due to low ridership and the high cost of installing [high-level platforms](/wiki/High-level_platform "High-level platform") for the then-new C3 railcars.[[53]](#cite_note-53) Elmont's eastbound platform officially opened in November 2021,[[54]](#cite_note-54) while the westbound platform opened in October 2022.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

The LIRR operates out of four western terminals in New York City. These terminals are:

* [Pennsylvania Station](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Station_(New_York_City) "Pennsylvania Station (New York City)"), in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"), is the busiest of the western terminals, serving almost 500 daily trains.[[10]](#cite_note-abouttheLIRR-10) It is reached via the [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak")-owned [East River Tunnels](/wiki/East_River_Tunnels "East River Tunnels") (the only LIRR-used trackage not owned by the LIRR) from the Main Line at [Harold Interlocking](/wiki/Harold_Interlocking "Harold Interlocking") in Long Island City. The New York City Subway's [34th Street–Penn Station (IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line)](/wiki/34th_Street%E2%80%93Penn_Station_(IRT_Broadway%E2%80%93Seventh_Avenue_Line) "34th Street–Penn Station (IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line)") ([1](/wiki/1_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "1 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[2](/wiki/2_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "2 (New York City Subway service)"), and ​[3](/wiki/3_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "3 (New York City Subway service)") trains) and [34th Street–Penn Station (IND Eighth Avenue Line)](/wiki/34th_Street%E2%80%93Penn_Station_(IND_Eighth_Avenue_Line) "34th Street–Penn Station (IND Eighth Avenue Line)") ([A](/wiki/A_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "A (New York City Subway service)"), ​[C](/wiki/C_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "C (New York City Subway service)"), and ​[E](/wiki/E_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "E (New York City Subway service)") trains) stations are adjacent to the terminal. It also connects LIRR with Amtrak and [NJ Transit](/wiki/NJ_Transit "NJ Transit") trains.
* [Grand Central Madison](/wiki/Grand_Central_Madison_station "Grand Central Madison station") is located under [Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal") and was built as part of the [East Side Access](/wiki/East_Side_Access "East Side Access") project.[[56]](#cite_note-esa-56) Service to the new terminal began on January 25, 2023.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Provision was made for this route on the lower level of the [63rd Street Tunnel](/wiki/63rd_Street_Tunnel "63rd Street Tunnel") under the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River"), which carries the New York City Subway's [IND 63rd Street Line](/wiki/IND_63rd_Street_Line "IND 63rd Street Line") ([F](/wiki/F_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "F (New York City Subway service)") and ​[M](/wiki/M_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "M (New York City Subway service)") trains) on its upper level. The East Side Access project is expected to reduce congestion while increasing the number of trains during peak hours.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) It serves as the primary terminal for the Hempstead Branch and serves all other electrified branches (as the LIRR's diesel fleet has a [loading gauge](/wiki/Loading_gauge "Loading gauge") too large for the 63rd Street Tunnel).[[60]](#cite_note-liw010620-60)
* [Atlantic Terminal](/wiki/Atlantic_Terminal "Atlantic Terminal"), formerly known as Flatbush Avenue, in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn") serves the [West Hempstead Branch](/wiki/West_Hempstead_Branch "West Hempstead Branch"), with limited service on other branches during the weekday peak. Other trains run as shuttles to Jamaica.[[10]](#cite_note-abouttheLIRR-10) It is next to the New York City Subway's [Atlantic Avenue–Barclays Center](/wiki/Atlantic_Avenue%E2%80%93Barclays_Center_(New_York_City_Subway) "Atlantic Avenue–Barclays Center (New York City Subway)") station complex ([2](/wiki/2_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "2 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[3](/wiki/3_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "3 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[4](/wiki/4_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "4 (New York City Subway service)"), ​[5](/wiki/5_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "5 (New York City Subway service)")​, [B](/wiki/B_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "B (New York City Subway service)"), ​[D](/wiki/D_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "D (New York City Subway service)"), [N](/wiki/N_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "N (New York City Subway service)"), ​[Q](/wiki/Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Q (New York City Subway service)")​, [R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)") and ​[W](/wiki/W_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "W (New York City Subway service)")​ trains), providing easy access to [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan"). With the opening of East Side Access, service between Atlantic Terminal and Jamaica is served mostly by shuttles.
* [Long Island City](/wiki/Long_Island_City_station "Long Island City station") - certain rush-hour trains run to one of two stations in [Long Island City, Queens](/wiki/Long_Island_City,_Queens "Long Island City, Queens"): the Long Island City station on the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River"), which is the oldest western terminal of the LIRR, or the [Hunterspoint Avenue](/wiki/Hunterspoint_Avenue_station_(LIRR) "Hunterspoint Avenue station (LIRR)") station, which is 0.6 miles to the east.[[10]](#cite_note-abouttheLIRR-10) From Hunterspoint Avenue, the [Hunters Point Avenue subway station](/wiki/Hunters_Point_Avenue_(IRT_Flushing_Line) "Hunters Point Avenue (IRT Flushing Line)") ([7](/wiki/7_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "7 (New York City Subway service)") and [<7>](/wiki/7d_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "7d (New York City Subway service)")​ trains) can be reached. The Long Island City station is near the [Vernon Boulevard–Jackson Avenue](/wiki/Vernon_Boulevard%E2%80%93Jackson_Avenue_(IRT_Flushing_Line) "Vernon Boulevard–Jackson Avenue (IRT Flushing Line)") subway station, also served by the 7 and <7>​ trains, and the Long Island City station also connects to the NYC Ferry's [East River Ferry](/wiki/East_River_Ferry "East River Ferry") to Midtown or Lower Manhattan.

In addition, the [Jamaica station](/wiki/Jamaica_station "Jamaica station") is a major [hub station](/wiki/Transport_hub "Transport hub") and transfer point in [Jamaica, Queens](/wiki/Jamaica,_Queens "Jamaica, Queens"). It has ten tracks and six platforms, plus [yard](/wiki/Rail_yard "Rail yard") and bypass tracks. Passengers can transfer between trains on all LIRR lines except the [Port Washington Branch](/wiki/Port_Washington_Branch "Port Washington Branch").[[10]](#cite_note-abouttheLIRR-10) The sixth platform opened in February 2020, and exclusively serves [Atlantic Branch](/wiki/Atlantic_Branch "Atlantic Branch") shuttle trains to Brooklyn.[[33]](#cite_note-MTA-LIRR-Hicksville-2016-33) Transfer is also made to separate facilities for three subway services at the [Sutphin Boulevard–Archer Avenue–JFK Airport station](/wiki/Sutphin_Boulevard%E2%80%93Archer_Avenue%E2%80%93JFK_Airport_station "Sutphin Boulevard–Archer Avenue–JFK Airport station") ([E](/wiki/E_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "E (New York City Subway service)")​, ​[J](/wiki/J_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "J (New York City Subway service)"), and ​[Z](/wiki/Z_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Z (New York City Subway service)") trains), a number of bus routes, and the [AirTrain](/wiki/AirTrain_JFK "AirTrain JFK") automated [people mover](/wiki/People_mover "People mover") to [JFK Airport](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_International_Airport "John F. Kennedy International Airport").[[61]](#cite_note-61) The railroad's headquarters are next to the station.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

A schematic of the LIRR's routes and fare zones. This schematic is not to scale.

The Long Island Rail Road system has eleven passenger branches, three of which are main trunk lines:[[63]](#cite_note-:5-63)

* [Main Line](/wiki/Main_Line_(Long_Island_Rail_Road) "Main Line (Long Island Rail Road)"), running along the middle of the island, between [Long Island City](/wiki/Long_Island_City_station "Long Island City station") and [Greenport](/wiki/Greenport_station "Greenport station"), via Jamaica.[[63]](#cite_note-:5-63)
* [Montauk Branch](/wiki/Montauk_Branch "Montauk Branch"), running along the southern edge of the island, between Long Island City and [Montauk](/wiki/Montauk_station "Montauk station"), via Jamaica.[[63]](#cite_note-:5-63)
* [Atlantic Branch](/wiki/Atlantic_Branch "Atlantic Branch"), running mostly in New York City to the south of both the Main Line and Montauk Branch, between Atlantic Terminal and [Valley Stream](/wiki/Valley_Stream_station "Valley Stream station"), via Jamaica.[[63]](#cite_note-:5-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)

There are eight minor branches.[[63]](#cite_note-:5-63) For scheduling and advertising purposes some of these branches are divided into sections; this is the case with the Montauk Branch, which is known as the [Babylon Branch](/wiki/Babylon_Branch "Babylon Branch") service in the electrified portion of the line between Jamaica and Babylon, while the diesel service beyond Babylon to Montauk is referred to as Montauk Branch service. All branches except the [Port Washington Branch](/wiki/Port_Washington_Branch "Port Washington Branch") pass through [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica_(LIRR_station) "Jamaica (LIRR station)"); the trackage west of Jamaica (except the [Port Washington Branch](/wiki/Port_Washington_Branch "Port Washington Branch")) is known as the [City Terminal Zone](/wiki/City_Terminal_Zone "City Terminal Zone"). The City Terminal Zone includes portions of the Main Line, Atlantic, and Montauk Branches, as well as the [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak")-owned [East River Tunnels](/wiki/East_River_Tunnels "East River Tunnels") to Penn Station.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### Current branches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Current branches")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/LIRR_Diesel_Regions_Map.jpg/330px-LIRR_Diesel_Regions_Map.jpg)](/wiki/File:LIRR_Diesel_Regions_Map.jpg)

A map of diesel territory on the Long Island Rail Road

* The Main Line runs from [Long Island City](/wiki/Long_Island_City_station "Long Island City station") east to [Greenport](/wiki/Greenport_station "Greenport station"). It is electrified west of the [Ronkonkoma station](/wiki/Ronkonkoma_station "Ronkonkoma station"); limited diesel train service runs from this point to the [Yaphank](/wiki/Yaphank_station "Yaphank station"), [Riverhead](/wiki/Riverhead_station "Riverhead station"), or Greenport stations. Trains using the East River Tunnels from Penn Station join the line at [Sunnyside Yard](/wiki/Sunnyside_Yard "Sunnyside Yard"). The services that run along this line are named after the branches they use; trains beyond Hicksville, where the Port Jefferson Branch diverges, are known as [Ronkonkoma Branch](/wiki/Ronkonkoma_Branch "Ronkonkoma Branch") and Greenport Branch trains.
* The Montauk Branch runs from Long Island City east to the [Montauk station](/wiki/Montauk_station "Montauk station"), with junctions with the Main Line at Long Island City and [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica_station "Jamaica station"). It is electrified from Jamaica east to [Babylon](/wiki/Babylon_station "Babylon station"). Trains operating east of Babylon are listed as Montauk Branch service and are hauled by diesel locomotives, while trains using the line from Jamaica to Babylon are labeled as [Babylon Branch](/wiki/Babylon_Branch "Babylon Branch") trains. The portion of the line between Long Island City and Jamaica, known as the Lower Montauk Branch, no longer carries passenger trains and is used only for freight service.
* The electrified Atlantic Branch runs from [Atlantic Terminal](/wiki/Atlantic_Terminal "Atlantic Terminal") in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn") east to Jamaica, where it meets the Main Line and the Montauk Branch, and then heads southeast to become the Long Beach Branch east of [Valley Stream](/wiki/Valley_Stream_station "Valley Stream station"). East of Valley Stream, the [Far Rockaway Branch](/wiki/Far_Rockaway_Branch "Far Rockaway Branch") turns south, while the [West Hempstead Branch](/wiki/West_Hempstead_Branch "West Hempstead Branch") turns northward.
* The electrified [Port Washington Branch](/wiki/Port_Washington_Branch "Port Washington Branch"), the only one that does not serve Jamaica, branches from the Main Line west of [Woodside](/wiki/Woodside_station_(LIRR) "Woodside station (LIRR)") (running parallel to the Main Line until [Winfield Junction](/wiki/Winfield_Junction_station "Winfield Junction station"), which is east of that station) and runs east to [Port Washington](/wiki/Port_Washington_station "Port Washington station"). It only serves four stations in [Nassau County](/wiki/Nassau_County,_New_York "Nassau County, New York"). It includes the [Manhasset Viaduct](/wiki/Manhasset_Viaduct "Manhasset Viaduct"), which is the highest bridge on the LIRR network.[[66]](#cite_note-66)
* The [Port Jefferson Branch](/wiki/Port_Jefferson_Branch "Port Jefferson Branch") splits from the Main Line east of Hicksville, with electric service to [Huntington](/wiki/Huntington_station_(LIRR) "Huntington station (LIRR)"). The section from Huntington to [Port Jefferson](/wiki/Port_Jefferson_station_(LIRR) "Port Jefferson station (LIRR)") is served only by diesel trains. Until 1938, it continued east to [Wading River](/wiki/Wading_River_station "Wading River station").[[67]](#cite_note-steel-67): 258
* The electrified [Hempstead Branch](/wiki/Hempstead_Branch "Hempstead Branch") splits from the Main Line east of [Elmont](/wiki/Elmont_station "Elmont station") (running parallel to the Main Line until just after [Floral Park](/wiki/Floral_Park_station "Floral Park station")) and runs east to [Hempstead](/wiki/Rosa_Parks_Hempstead_Transit_Center "Rosa Parks Hempstead Transit Center"). At [Garden City](/wiki/Garden_City_station_(LIRR) "Garden City station (LIRR)"), the [Garden City–Mitchel Field Secondary](/wiki/Garden_City%E2%80%93Mitchel_Field_Secondary "Garden City–Mitchel Field Secondary") curves off and goes to [Mitchel Field](/wiki/Mitchel_Field "Mitchel Field").
* The electrified [West Hempstead Branch](/wiki/West_Hempstead_Branch "West Hempstead Branch") splits from the Montauk Branch east of the Valley Stream station and runs northeast to [West Hempstead](/wiki/West_Hempstead_station "West Hempstead station"), originally continuing to junctions with the Hempstead Branch and the Oyster Bay Branch at the Main Line.
* The [Oyster Bay Branch](/wiki/Oyster_Bay_Branch "Oyster Bay Branch") splits from the Main Line east of [Mineola](/wiki/Mineola_station_(LIRR) "Mineola station (LIRR)") and heads north and east to [Oyster Bay](/wiki/Oyster_Bay_station "Oyster Bay station"). The first section to [East Williston](/wiki/East_Williston_station "East Williston station") is electrified;[[68]](#cite_note-68) only diesel trains run along the majority of the line to Oyster Bay.[[69]](#cite_note-LIRR_Stations_p._57-69)
* The diesel-only [Central Branch](/wiki/Central_Branch_(Long_Island_Rail_Road) "Central Branch (Long Island Rail Road)") runs southeast from the Main Line east of [Bethpage](/wiki/Bethpage_station "Bethpage station") to the Montauk Branch west of the Babylon station, giving an alternate route to the Montauk Branch east of Babylon. The Central Branch used to continue west from Bethpage to include what is now the Garden City–Mitchel Field Secondary. It was proposed to be electrified as part of the 2020–2024 MTA Capital Program.[[70]](#cite_note-:2-70)
* The electrified [Far Rockaway Branch](/wiki/Far_Rockaway_Branch "Far Rockaway Branch") splits from the Atlantic Branch east of the Valley Stream station and runs south and southwest to [Far Rockaway](/wiki/Far_Rockaway_station_(LIRR) "Far Rockaway station (LIRR)"). It used to connect westward to what is now the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway")'s [IND Rockaway Line](/wiki/IND_Rockaway_Line "IND Rockaway Line") to the [Hammels](/wiki/Hammels,_Queens "Hammels, Queens") and [Rockaway Park](/wiki/Rockaway_Park,_Queens "Rockaway Park, Queens") neighborhoods of Queens.
* The electrified [Long Beach Branch](/wiki/Long_Beach_Branch "Long Beach Branch") begins where the Atlantic Branch ends east of the Valley Stream station (running parallel to the Montauk Branch until just after [Lynbrook](/wiki/Lynbrook_station "Lynbrook station")) where it turns south to end at [Long Beach](/wiki/Long_Beach_station_(LIRR) "Long Beach station (LIRR)").

### Former branches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Former branches")]

The railroad has dropped a number of branches due to lack of ridership over the years. Part of the [Rockaway Beach Branch](/wiki/Rockaway_Beach_Branch "Rockaway Beach Branch") became part of the [IND Rockaway Line](/wiki/IND_Rockaway_Line "IND Rockaway Line") of the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway"), while others were downgraded to freight branches, and the rest [abandoned](/wiki/Abandoned_railway "Abandoned railway") entirely. Additionally, the Long Island Rail Road operated trains over portions of the [Brooklyn Rapid Transit (BRT)](/wiki/Brooklyn_Rapid_Transit_Company "Brooklyn Rapid Transit Company") elevated and subway lines until 1917.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

* The [Bethpage Branch](/wiki/Bethpage_Branch "Bethpage Branch") ran north from the Main Line and Central Branch at [Bethpage](/wiki/Bethpage,_New_York "Bethpage, New York").[[72]](#cite_note-72)
* The [Bushwick Branch](/wiki/Bushwick_Branch "Bushwick Branch"), also called the Bushwick Lead Track, is a freight railroad branch that runs from Bushwick, Brooklyn, to Fresh Pond Junction in Queens, where it connects with the Montauk Branch.
* The Camp Upton Branch was a short branch north from the Main Line to [Camp Upton](/wiki/Camp_Upton "Camp Upton").[[73]](#cite_note-73)
* The [Cedarhurst Cut-off](/wiki/Cedarhurst_Cut-off "Cedarhurst Cut-off"), officially known as the **New York and Rockaway Railroad**, was an extension of the [Montauk Branch](/wiki/Montauk_Branch "Montauk Branch") from its merger with the [Atlantic Branch](/wiki/Atlantic_Branch "Atlantic Branch") at [Springfield Junction](/wiki/Springfield_Junction_(Long_Island_Rail_Road) "Springfield Junction (Long Island Rail Road)") to [Cedarhurst](/wiki/Cedarhurst,_New_York "Cedarhurst, New York"), where it would turn west and run parallel to the [Far Rockaway Branch](/wiki/Far_Rockaway_Branch "Far Rockaway Branch") until reaching Mott Avenue in [Far Rockaway](/wiki/Far_Rockaway,_Queens "Far Rockaway, Queens").
* The [Central Extension](/wiki/Central_Extension_(Long_Island_Rail_Road) "Central Extension (Long Island Rail Road)") ran from Garden City eastward to Central Park (¾ mile south of current Bethpage station) and as far east as Bethpage Junction. The line was cut back to the point where it stopped at Island Trees. Today the western part of track still in use for freight and storage, and is officially known today as the [Garden City Secondary](/wiki/Garden_City_Secondary "Garden City Secondary").
* The Chestnut Street Incline (Brooklyn) between Atlantic Avenue and Fulton Street was opened in 1898 to allow for thru-operation over the Jamaica/Broadway Elevated Line to the East River ferry terminal. In 1909 thru passenger service to Manhattan via the [Williamsburg Bridge](/wiki/Williamsburg_Bridge "Williamsburg Bridge") was established in coordination with the [Brooklyn Rapid Transit Company](/wiki/Brooklyn_Rapid_Transit_Company "Brooklyn Rapid Transit Company") (BRT). LIRR Passenger service operated to Chambers Street between May 1909 and September 1917.
* The [Creedmoor Branch](/wiki/Creedmoor_Branch "Creedmoor Branch"), a remnant of the [Central Railroad of Long Island](/wiki/Central_Railroad_of_Long_Island "Central Railroad of Long Island") (CRRLI) of [Alexander Turney Stewart](/wiki/Alexander_Turney_Stewart "Alexander Turney Stewart"), was a short branch from the Main Line at [Floral Park](/wiki/Floral_Park,_NY "Floral Park, NY") northwest through [Creedmoor](/wiki/Creedmoor,_NY "Creedmoor, NY"). It once went as far northwest as [Flushing](/wiki/Flushing,_Queens "Flushing, Queens").
* The [Evergreen Branch](/wiki/Evergreen_Branch "Evergreen Branch") connected the [Bushwick Branch](/wiki/Bushwick_Branch "Bushwick Branch") east of [Bushwick Terminal](/wiki/Bushwick_Terminal "Bushwick Terminal") with the [Bay Ridge Branch](/wiki/Bay_Ridge_Branch "Bay Ridge Branch") north of [East New York](/wiki/East_New_York,_Brooklyn "East New York, Brooklyn").
* The [Flushing Bay Freight Spur](/wiki/Flushing_Bay_Freight_Spur "Flushing Bay Freight Spur") extended north from the [Whitestone Branch](/wiki/Whitestone_Branch "Whitestone Branch"), then across the [Woodside Branch](/wiki/Woodside_Branch "Woodside Branch") and then the connecting line between both branches before terminating along the south coast of [Flushing Bay](/wiki/Flushing_Bay "Flushing Bay").[[74]](#cite_note-auto-74)
* The [Glendale Cut-off](/wiki/Glendale_Cut-off "Glendale Cut-off") ran south from the Main Line at [Rego Park](/wiki/Rego_Park,_Queens "Rego Park, Queens") to the Montauk Branch at [Glendale](/wiki/Glendale,_Queens "Glendale, Queens"). There it became the [Rockaway Beach Branch](/wiki/Rockaway_Beach_Branch "Rockaway Beach Branch"), running south across [Jamaica Bay](/wiki/Jamaica_Bay "Jamaica Bay") to [Hammels](/wiki/Hammels,_Queens "Hammels, Queens") and west to [Rockaway Park](/wiki/Rockaway_Park,_Queens "Rockaway Park, Queens"). The Rockaway Beach Branch south of [Ozone Park](/wiki/Ozone_Park,_Queens "Ozone Park, Queens") is now the [IND Rockaway Line](/wiki/IND_Rockaway_Line "IND Rockaway Line") of the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway").
* The [Manhattan Beach Branch](/wiki/Manhattan_Beach_Branch "Manhattan Beach Branch") ran south from the Bay Ridge Branch at [Flatbush](/wiki/Flatbush,_Brooklyn "Flatbush, Brooklyn") to [Manhattan Beach](/wiki/Manhattan_Beach,_New_York "Manhattan Beach, New York").
* The [Manorville Branch](/wiki/Manorville_Branch "Manorville Branch") or Manor Branch ran from the Main Line at [Manorville](/wiki/Manorville,_New_York "Manorville, New York") southeast to the Montauk Branch at [Eastport](/wiki/Eastport,_New_York "Eastport, New York"). It was originally part of the Sag Harbor Branch (*See below*).
* The [Mineola-West Hempstead Branch](/w/index.php?title=Mineola-West_Hempstead_Branch&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mineola-West Hempstead Branch (page does not exist)") ran north of the terminus of the [West Hempstead Branch](/wiki/West_Hempstead_Branch "West Hempstead Branch") across [NY 24](/wiki/NY_24 "NY 24") to [Country Life Press Station](/wiki/Country_Life_Press_(LIRR_station) "Country Life Press (LIRR station)") where it briefly joined the [Hempstead Branch](/wiki/Hempstead_Branch "Hempstead Branch") then ran north of the [Garden City Secondary](/wiki/Garden_City_Secondary "Garden City Secondary") towards a wye at [Mineola Station](/wiki/Mineola_(LIRR_station) "Mineola (LIRR station)") with one branch that terminated at the station and another that crossed the main line and ended near the southern terminus of the [Oyster Bay Branch](/wiki/Oyster_Bay_Branch "Oyster Bay Branch").
* The [Montauk Cut-off](/wiki/Montauk_Cut-off "Montauk Cut-off") was a short connecting track between the Lower Montauk Branch and the Main Line in Long Island City that allowed trains to change direction without entering the [Long Island City station](/wiki/Long_Island_City_station "Long Island City station").[[75]](#cite_note-75)
* The [Northport Branch](/wiki/Northport_Branch "Northport Branch") ran northeast of the current [Port Jefferson Branch](/wiki/Port_Jefferson_Branch "Port Jefferson Branch") between [Greenlawn](/wiki/Greenlawn,_New_York "Greenlawn, New York") and [Northport Village](/wiki/Northport,_New_York "Northport, New York").[[76]](#cite_note-76)
* The [North Shore Freight Branch](/wiki/North_Shore_Freight_Branch "North Shore Freight Branch") ran from the Main Line at [Sunnyside Yard](/wiki/Sunnyside_Yard "Sunnyside Yard") west to the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") where [Gantry Plaza State Park](/wiki/Gantry_Plaza_State_Park "Gantry Plaza State Park") is now.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78) Originally built by the [Flushing and North Side Railroad](/wiki/Flushing_and_North_Side_Railroad "Flushing and North Side Railroad"), some of the surviving right-of-way can be found at the Arch Street Shops within the Sunnyside Yard.
* The [Roosevelt Field Spur](/w/index.php?title=Roosevelt_Field_Spur&action=edit&redlink=1 "Roosevelt Field Spur (page does not exist)") branched off northward from the current [Garden City Secondary](/wiki/Garden_City_Secondary "Garden City Secondary") just north of Commercial Avenue. From there, it crossed Stewart Avenue just west of present-day South Street before turning slightly northeast, crossing over the Meadowbrook Parkway. The overpass, as well as sections along the sidewalk on South Street, can still be seen today. From there, it continued north before curving east and coming to an end near Zeckendorf Boulevard. The line was used for freight only.
* The [Sag Harbor Branch](/wiki/Sag_Harbor_Branch "Sag Harbor Branch") ran north from the Montauk Branch at [Bridgehampton](/wiki/Bridgehampton,_New_York "Bridgehampton, New York") to [Sag Harbor](/wiki/Sag_Harbor,_New_York "Sag Harbor, New York").
* The [Wading River Branch](/wiki/Wading_River_Branch "Wading River Branch") ran east from Port Jefferson to [Wading River](/wiki/Wading_River,_New_York "Wading River, New York"), serving the towns of [Mount Sinai](/wiki/Mount_Sinai,_New_York "Mount Sinai, New York"), [Miller Place](/wiki/Miller_Place,_New_York "Miller Place, New York"), [Rocky Point](/wiki/Rocky_Point,_New_York "Rocky Point, New York"), and [Shoreham](/wiki/Shoreham,_New_York "Shoreham, New York").[[79]](#cite_note-79)
* The [White Line](/wiki/White_Line_(Long_Island_Rail_Road) "White Line (Long Island Rail Road)"), which was built by the LIRR subsidiary **Newtown and Flushing Railroad** ran south of the Port Washington Branch between [Winfield Junction](/wiki/Winfield_Junction_(LIRR_station) "Winfield Junction (LIRR station)") and [Flushing](/wiki/Flushing_Main_Street_(LIRR_station) "Flushing Main Street (LIRR station)") between 1873 and 1876.[[80]](#cite_note-80)
* The [Whitestone Branch](/wiki/Whitestone_Branch "Whitestone Branch"), which was originally built by the [Flushing and North Side Railroad](/wiki/Flushing_and_North_Side_Railroad "Flushing and North Side Railroad") (F&NS), split from the Port Washington Branch near [Mets–Willets Point station](/wiki/Mets%E2%80%93Willets_Point_station_(LIRR) "Mets–Willets Point station (LIRR)") and ran north and east to [Whitestone](/wiki/Whitestone,_New_York "Whitestone, New York").
* The [Woodside Branch](/wiki/Woodside_Branch "Woodside Branch") ran north of the current Port Washington Branch between [Woodside](/wiki/Woodside,_Queens "Woodside, Queens") and east of the present [Corona Yard](/wiki/Corona_Yard "Corona Yard") west of the [Flushing River](/wiki/Flushing_River "Flushing River"). It also had a connecting spur to the Whitestone Branch.

The Mets-Willets Point station in 2007

In addition to its daily commuter patronage, the LIRR also offers the following services:

* The railroad operates extra trains during the summer season that cater to the Long Island beach trade. Special package ticket deals are offered to places like [Long Beach](/wiki/Long_Beach,_New_York "Long Beach, New York"), [Jones Beach](/wiki/Jones_Beach_State_Park "Jones Beach State Park"), [the Hamptons](/wiki/The_Hamptons "The Hamptons"), [Montauk](/wiki/Montauk,_New_York "Montauk, New York"), and [Greenport](/wiki/Greenport,_Suffolk_County,_New_York "Greenport, Suffolk County, New York"). Some of these packages require bus and ferry connections.
* The railroad operates extra trains to and from [Atlantic Terminal](/wiki/Atlantic_Terminal "Atlantic Terminal") for [Brooklyn Nets](/wiki/Brooklyn_Nets "Brooklyn Nets") home games at [Barclays Center](/wiki/Barclays_Center "Barclays Center").[[90]](#cite_note-90)

Additionally, on the day of the [Belmont Stakes](/wiki/Belmont_Stakes "Belmont Stakes") horse race the railroad runs extra trains to accommodate the large number of spectators attending the event.[[91]](#cite_note-91)
* One special non-passenger service offered by the railroad was the yearly operation of the [Ringling Brothers Barnum and Bailey Circus](/wiki/Ringling_Brothers_Barnum_and_Bailey_Circus "Ringling Brothers Barnum and Bailey Circus") train between Long Island City and [Nassau Veterans Memorial Coliseum](/wiki/Nassau_Veterans_Memorial_Coliseum "Nassau Veterans Memorial Coliseum") in Uniondale. Highly publicized by the LIRR, this event drew large crowds of spectators.[[92]](#cite_note-youtube.com-92) With Ringling Bros. Barnum and Bailey's closure, this was discontinued in May 2017.

## Fare structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Fare structure")]

Like [Metro-North Railroad](/wiki/Metro-North_Railroad "Metro-North Railroad") and NJ Transit, the Long Island Rail Road fare system is based on the distance a passenger travels, as opposed to the New York City Subway and the area's bus systems, which charge a flat rate. The railroad is broken up into eight non-consecutively numbered fare zones. Zone 1, the [City Terminal Zone](/wiki/City_Terminal_Zone "City Terminal Zone"), includes Penn Station, Grand Central, all stations in Brooklyn, all stations in Queens west of Jamaica on the Main Line, and Mets–Willets Point.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

Zone 3 includes Jamaica as well as all other stations in eastern Queens except Far Rockaway.[[98]](#cite_note-mta.info-98) Zones 4 and 7 include all stations in Nassau County, plus Far Rockaway and Belmont Park in Queens.[[98]](#cite_note-mta.info-98) Zones 9, 10, 12 and 14 include all stations in Suffolk County.[[98]](#cite_note-mta.info-98) Each zone contains many stations, and the same fare applies for travel between any station in the origin zone and any station in the destination zone.[[98]](#cite_note-mta.info-98)

[![LIRR ticket vending machines, as seen at the Bethpage station.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Bethpage_LIRR_Station%3B_2018-10-19%3B_19.jpg/250px-Bethpage_LIRR_Station%3B_2018-10-19%3B_19.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bethpage_LIRR_Station;_2018-10-19;_19.jpg)

LIRR ticket vending machines at the Bethpage station

### Peak and off-peak fares

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Peak and off-peak fares")]

Peak fares are charged during the week on trains that arrive at western terminals between 6 AM and 10 AM, and for trains that depart from western terminals between 4 PM and 8 PM.[[99]](#cite_note-99) Any passenger holding an off-peak ticket on a peak train is required to pay a step up fee.[[100]](#cite_note-web.mta.info-100) Passengers can buy tickets from ticket agents or ticket vending machines (TVMs) or on the train from conductors, but will incur an on-board penalty fee for doing so.[[100]](#cite_note-web.mta.info-100) This fee is waived for customers boarding at a station without a ticket office or ticket machine, senior citizens, people with disabilities or Medicare customers.[[100]](#cite_note-web.mta.info-100)

There are several types of tickets: one way, round trip, peak, off-peak, AM peak or off-peak senior/disabled, peak child, and off-peak child. On off-peak trains, passengers can buy a family ticket for children who are accompanied by an 18-year-old for $0.75 if bought from the station agent or TVM, $1.00 on the train. Senior citizen/disabled passengers traveling during the morning peak hours are required to pay the AM peak senior citizen/disabled rate. This rate is not charged during PM peak hours.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

Commuters can also buy a peak or off-peak ten trip ride, a weekly unlimited or an unlimited monthly pass.[[102]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-102) Monthly passes are good on any train regardless of the time of day, within the fare zones specified on the pass.[[102]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-102)

The LIRR charged off-peak fares at all times during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[103]](#cite_note-103) Peak fares were reinstated on March 1, 2022, and several new discounts and ticket options were introduced at the same time.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

### Special fares

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Special fares")]

During the summer the railroad offers special summer package ticket deals to places such as Long Beach, Jones Beach, the Hamptons, Montauk, and Greenport. Passengers traveling to the Hamptons and Montauk on the *Cannonball* can reserve a seat in the all-reserved Parlor Cars.[[105]](#cite_note-MTA_2015-105)

Prior to November 2021, passengers going to Belmont Park had to buy a special ticket to go from Jamaica to Belmont Park (or vice versa). Weekly and monthly passes were not accepted at Belmont Park.[[106]](#cite_note-mta.info1-106) With the opening of [Elmont station](/wiki/Elmont_station "Elmont station") in November 2021, Belmont Park and Elmont were placed into fare zone 4.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

### CityTicket

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: CityTicket")]

In 2003, the LIRR and Metro-North started a pilot program in which passengers traveling within New York City were allowed to buy one-way tickets for $2.50.[[108]](#cite_note-Lisi_2003-108) The special reduced-fare CityTicket, proposed by the New York City Transit Riders Council,[[108]](#cite_note-Lisi_2003-108) was formally introduced in 2004.[[109]](#cite_note-109) The discounted fares were initially only available for travel on Saturdays and Sundays.[[110]](#cite_note-110) In March 2022, it was expanded to include all off-peak trains throughout the week for $5.[[111]](#cite_note-111) The MTA announced plans in December 2022 to allow CityTickets to be used on peak trains as well;[[112]](#cite_note-Feldman_2022-112)[[113]](#cite_note-Castillo_2022-113) governor [Kathy Hochul](/wiki/Kathy_Hochul "Kathy Hochul") confirmed these plans the next month.[[114]](#cite_note-Martinez_2023-114) The peak CityTickets, as announced in July 2023, would cost $7 each.[[115]](#cite_note-Kaye_2023_g749-115) As part of a one-year pilot program starting in July 2024, monthly tickets for LIRR trips entirely within New York City would also receive a 10% discount.[[116]](#cite_note-CBS_New_York_2024_c370-116)[[117]](#cite_note-Simko-Bednarski_2024_p168-117)

CityTicket is valid for travel within Zones 1 and 3 on the Long Island Rail Road. CityTickets can only be bought before boarding – except at [Mets–Willets Point](/wiki/Mets%E2%80%93Willets_Point_station_(LIRR) "Mets–Willets Point station (LIRR)"), where they can be purchased on board; they must be used on the day of purchase.[[106]](#cite_note-mta.info1-106)

CityTicket is not valid for travel to the [Elmont](/wiki/Elmont_station "Elmont station") station (located in Nassau County, just east of the Queens-Nassau border) – or the [Far Rockaway](/wiki/Far_Rockaway_station_(LIRR) "Far Rockaway station (LIRR)") or special event-only [Belmont Park](/wiki/Belmont_Park_(LIRR_station) "Belmont Park (LIRR station)") station (located in Queens, just west of the Queens-Nassau border) – and are all within Zone 4.[[106]](#cite_note-mta.info1-106)

### Freedom Ticket

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Freedom Ticket")]

In late 2017, the MTA was slated to launch a pilot that will allow LIRR, bus and subway service to use one ticket.[[118]](#cite_note-amny20170208-118) The proposal for the ticket, called the "Freedom Ticket," was initially put forth by the New York City Transit Riders Council (NYCTRC) in 2007.[[119]](#cite_note-pcac201512-119): 1 The NYCTRC wrote a [proof of concept](/wiki/Proof_of_concept "Proof of concept") report in 2015.[[120]](#cite_note-amny20151202-120) At the time of the report, [express bus](/wiki/List_of_express_bus_routes_in_New_York_City "List of express bus routes in New York City") riders from Southeast Queens had some of the longest commutes in the city, with their commutes being 96 minutes long, yet they paid a premium fare of $6.50.[[119]](#cite_note-pcac201512-119)

Riders who take the [dollar van](/wiki/Dollar_vans_in_the_New_York_metropolitan_area "Dollar vans in the New York metropolitan area") to the subway paid $4.75 to get to Manhattan in 65 minutes; riders who only took the bus and subway paid $2.75 to get to Manhattan in 86 minutes; and riders who took the LIRR paid $10 to get to Manhattan in 35 minutes.[[119]](#cite_note-pcac201512-119): iii Unlike the CityTicket, the Freedom Ticket would be valid for off-peak and multidirectional travel; have free transfers to the subway and bus system; and be capped at $215 per month.[[119]](#cite_note-pcac201512-119): 1–2 At the time, monthly CityTickets cost $330 per month.[[120]](#cite_note-amny20151202-120)

The Freedom Ticket will initially be available for sale at the [Atlantic Terminal](/wiki/Atlantic_Terminal "Atlantic Terminal"), [Nostrand Avenue](/wiki/Nostrand_Avenue_(LIRR_station) "Nostrand Avenue (LIRR station)"), and [East New York](/wiki/East_New_York_(LIRR_station) "East New York (LIRR station)") stations in Brooklyn and at the [Laurelton](/wiki/Laurelton_(LIRR_station) "Laurelton (LIRR station)"), [Locust Manor](/wiki/Locust_Manor_(LIRR_station) "Locust Manor (LIRR station)"), [Rosedale](/wiki/Rosedale_(LIRR_station) "Rosedale (LIRR station)"), and [St. Albans](/wiki/St._Albans_(LIRR_station) "St. Albans (LIRR station)") stations in Queens.[[118]](#cite_note-amny20170208-118)[[121]](#cite_note-:0-121)[[122]](#cite_note-:1-122) Riders, under the pilot, would be able to purchase one-way, weekly, or monthly passes that will be valid on the LIRR, on buses, and the subway. The fare will be higher than the price of a ride on the [MetroCard](/wiki/MetroCard_(New_York_City) "MetroCard (New York City)"), but it will be lower than the combined price of an LIRR ticket and a MetroCard, and it will allow unlimited free transfers between the LIRR, buses, and subway.[[122]](#cite_note-:1-122)

The former head of the MTA, Thomas Prendergast, announced at the January 2017 board meeting that the plan would be explored in a field study to determine fares and the impact on existing service.[[118]](#cite_note-amny20170208-118) The plan is intended to fill approximately 20,000 unused seats of existing trains to Atlantic Terminal and Penn Station (or about 50% to 60% of peak trains in each direction),[[121]](#cite_note-:0-121) while at the same time providing affordable service to people with long commutes.[[122]](#cite_note-:1-122) The details were to be announced in spring 2017, and the pilot would last six months.[[118]](#cite_note-amny20170208-118)

The MTA Board voted to approve a six-month pilot for a similar concept, the Atlantic Ticket, in May 2018. The Atlantic Ticket is similar in that it would allow LIRR riders in southeast Queens to purchase a one-way ticket to or from Atlantic Terminal for $5. The Atlantic Ticket would start in June 2018.[[123]](#cite_note-123) The success of the pilot program has led the MTA to extend the program up to the summer of 2020 and renewed calls for the program to be implemented within New York City, where the fare for the Freedom Ticket—if approved—would cost US$2.75 and include free transfers between the LIRR & Metro-North, bus, and subway.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

### Far Rockaway Ticket

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Far Rockaway Ticket")]

In May 2023, the MTA announced that, as part of wider fare changes and in response to requests from [Far Rockaway](/wiki/Far_Rockaway "Far Rockaway") residents, a discounted ticket option – the Far Rockaway Ticket – would be introduced for travelers traveling on the [Far Rockaway Branch](/wiki/Far_Rockaway_Branch "Far Rockaway Branch") between Far Rockaway and other stations within New York City (excluding Belmont Park). This new ticket would provide the same discounts as a regular CityTicket, while also having protections against fare evasion, given [the unique nature of the Far Rockaway Branch's route](/wiki/Far_Rockaway_Branch#Stations "Far Rockaway Branch").[[125]](#cite_note-125)[[126]](#cite_note-:12-126)[[127]](#cite_note-:22-127) Tickets can only be purchased at the Far Rockaway station or on the MTA's TrainTime app in the station's vicinity; geolocation restrictions on the TrainTime app only allow purchase of discounted tickets within the vicinity of the Far Rockaway station.[[126]](#cite_note-:12-126)[[127]](#cite_note-:22-127)

The Far Rockaway Ticket became available for purchase on August 20, 2023.[[127]](#cite_note-:22-127)

### OMNY

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: OMNY")]

Main article: [OMNY](/wiki/OMNY "OMNY")

In 2017, it was announced that the [MetroCard](/wiki/MetroCard "MetroCard") fare payment system, used on New York City-area rapid transit and bus systems, would be phased out and replaced by [OMNY](/wiki/OMNY "OMNY"), a [contactless fare payment](/wiki/Contactless_payment "Contactless payment") system. Fare payment would be made using [Apple Pay](/wiki/Apple_Pay "Apple Pay"), [Google Pay](/wiki/Google_Pay_(payment_method) "Google Pay (payment method)"), debit/credit cards with [near-field communication](/wiki/Near-field_communication "Near-field communication") enabled, or radio-frequency identification cards.[[128]](#cite_note-128)[[129]](#cite_note-129) As part of the implementation of OMNY, the MTA also plans to use the system in the Long Island Rail Road and Metro-North Railroad.[[130]](#cite_note-130)

### Combo Ticket

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Combo Ticket")]

In December 2022, the MTA announced the launch of an additional fare for use on journeys that utilize both of its railroad systems via Grand Central. The fare is priced as $8 more than an adult off-peak ticket from an origin station on one system to Grand Central. It is valid on both peak and off-peak trains.[[131]](#cite_note-131)[[132]](#cite_note-132)

## Train operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Train operations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/LIRR_Train_Car_Interior.JPG/250px-LIRR_Train_Car_Interior.JPG)](/wiki/File:LIRR_Train_Car_Interior.JPG)

The interior of an [M7](/wiki/M7_(railcar) "M7 (railcar)") car

The LIRR is relatively isolated from the rest of the national rail system despite operating out of Penn Station, the nation's busiest rail terminal. It connects with other railroads in just two locations:

* West of [Harold Interlocking](/wiki/Harold_Interlocking "Harold Interlocking") in [Sunnyside, Queens](/wiki/Sunnyside,_Queens "Sunnyside, Queens"), LIRR trains enter the Amtrak-operated [Northeast Corridor](/wiki/Northeast_Corridor "Northeast Corridor") leading to the [East River Tunnels](/wiki/East_River_Tunnels "East River Tunnels"). When this track was owned by the [Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad "Pennsylvania Railroad"), trains of the PRR connected to the LIRR at Penn Station. During the 1920s and 1930s a through sleeper was carried by PRR and LIRR trains from Pittsburgh to Montauk, called the 'Sunrise Special'.[[133]](#cite_note-arrts-arrchives.com-133)[[134]](#cite_note-arrts-arrchives1927-134)
* In [Glendale, Queens](/wiki/Glendale,_Queens "Glendale, Queens"), the LIRR connects with CSX's [Fremont Secondary](/wiki/Fremont_Secondary "Fremont Secondary"), which leads to the [Hell Gate Bridge](/wiki/Hell_Gate_Bridge "Hell Gate Bridge") and [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England"); however, once trains leave the secondary, they enter LIRR trackage.[[135]](#cite_note-135)

All LIRR trains have an engineer (driver in non-US English) who operates the train, and a conductor who is responsible for the safe movement of the train, fare collection and on-board customer service. In addition, trains may have one or more assistant conductors to assist with fare collection and other duties. The LIRR is one of the last railroads in the United States to use mechanical interlocking control towers to regulate rail traffic.[[136]](#cite_note-136)

As of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit), the LIRR has 8 active control towers. All movements on the LIRR are under the control of the Movement Bureau in Jamaica, which gives orders to the towers that control a specific portion of the railroad. Movements in Amtrak territory are controlled by [Penn Station Control Center](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Station_(New_York_City)#Tracks_and_surrounding_infrastructure "Pennsylvania Station (New York City)") or PSCC, run jointly by the LIRR and Amtrak. The PSCC controls as far east as [Harold Interlocking](/wiki/Harold_Interlocking "Harold Interlocking"), in [Sunnyside, Queens](/wiki/Sunnyside,_Queens "Sunnyside, Queens"). The PSCC replaced several towers.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

The Jamaica Control Center, operational since the third quarter of 2010, controls the area around Jamaica terminal by direct control of interlockings. This replaced several towers in Jamaica including Jay and Hall towers at the west and east ends of Jamaica station respectively. At additional locations, line side towers control the various switches and signals in accordance with the timetable and under the direction of the Movement Bureau in Jamaica.[[138]](#cite_note-138)

### Signal and safety systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Signal and safety systems")]

Today's LIRR signal system has evolved from its legacy [Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad "Pennsylvania Railroad") (PRR)-based system, and the railroad utilizes a variety of wayside [railroad signals](/wiki/Railroad_signal "Railroad signal") including position light, color light and dwarf signals.[[139]](#cite_note-139) In addition, much of the LIRR is equipped with a bi-directional [Pulse code cab signaling](/wiki/Pulse_code_cab_signaling "Pulse code cab signaling") called [automatic speed control](/wiki/Automatic_train_control "Automatic train control") (ASC), though portions of the railway still retain single direction, wayside-only signaling. Unlike other railroads, which began using color-light signals in the 20th century, the LIRR did not begin using signals with color lights on its above-ground sections until 2006.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Some portions of the railway lack automatic signals and cab signals completely, instead train and track car movements are governed only by timetable and verbal/written [train orders](/wiki/Train_order_operation "Train order operation"), although these areas are gradually receiving modern signals.[[140]](#cite_note-140) Many other signals and switching systems on the LIRR are being modernized and upgraded as part of the Main Line's Third Track Project, most notably at Mineola, where the system is being completely redone and modernized.[[141]](#cite_note-141)

[![A Long Island Rail Road M9, showing both over-running and under-running third rail shoes.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/LIRR_Kawasaki_M9_EMU_-_Jamaica_NY.jpg/250px-LIRR_Kawasaki_M9_EMU_-_Jamaica_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:LIRR_Kawasaki_M9_EMU_-_Jamaica_NY.jpg)

A Long Island Rail Road M9, showing the railcar's dual-type contact shoe

On portions of the railroad equipped with ASC, engineers consult the speed display unit, which is capable of displaying seven speed indications. As a result of a December 1, 2013, [train derailment](/wiki/December_2013_Spuyten_Duyvil_derailment "December 2013 Spuyten Duyvil derailment") in [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx") on the Metro-North Railroad, railroads with similar cab signal systems to Metro-North, such as the LIRR, were ordered to modify the systems to enforce certain speed limit changes, which has resulted in lower average speeds and actual speed limits across the LIRR.[[142]](#cite_note-142)

### Power transmission

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Power transmission")]

The LIRR's electrified lines are powered via a [third rail](/wiki/Third_rail "Third rail") at [750 volts DC](/wiki/List_of_railway_electrification_systems#750_V_DC_conductor "List of railway electrification systems").[[143]](#cite_note-143)

## Rolling stock

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Rolling stock")]

Main article: [Long Island Rail Road rolling stock](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road_rolling_stock "Long Island Rail Road rolling stock")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/MTA_LIRR_train_leaving_Woodside.jpg/250px-MTA_LIRR_train_leaving_Woodside.jpg)](/wiki/File:MTA_LIRR_train_leaving_Woodside.jpg)

### Electric fleet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Electric fleet")]

The LIRR's electric fleet consists of 836 [M7](/wiki/M7_(railcar) "M7 (railcar)"), 170 [M3](/wiki/M3_(railcar) "M3 (railcar)"), and 202 [M9](/wiki/M9_(railcar) "M9 (railcar)")[[144]](#cite_note-144) [electric multiple unit](/wiki/Electric_multiple_unit "Electric multiple unit") cars in married pairs, meaning each car needs the other one to operate, with each car containing its own engineer's cab. The trainsets typically range from 6 to 12 cars long.

In September 2013, MTA announced that the LIRR would procure new [M9](/wiki/M9_(railcar) "M9 (railcar)") railcars from [Kawasaki](/wiki/Kawasaki_Heavy_Industries_Rolling_Stock_Company "Kawasaki Heavy Industries Rolling Stock Company").[[145]](#cite_note-145)[[146]](#cite_note-146) A 2014 MTA forecast indicated that the LIRR would need 416 M9 railcars; 180 to replace the outdated [M3](/wiki/M3_(railcar) "M3 (railcar)") railcars and an additional 236 railcars for the additional passengers expected once the East Side Access project is complete.[[147]](#cite_note-CPOC_Jul_2014-147) The first M9s entered revenue service on September 11, 2019.[[148]](#cite_note-148)[[149]](#cite_note-149)

On June 23, 2025, the MTA awarded the [M9A](/wiki/M9_(railcar) "M9 (railcar)") contract to [Alstom](/wiki/Alstom "Alstom"). 160 of the 316 cars being built will run on the LIRR.[[150]](#cite_note-150)[[151]](#cite_note-151)[[152]](#cite_note-152)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/LIRR_bi-level_coaches.jpg/250px-LIRR_bi-level_coaches.jpg)](/wiki/File:LIRR_bi-level_coaches.jpg)

[C3](/wiki/C3_(railcar) "C3 (railcar)") bi-level coaches at a [grade crossing](/wiki/Grade_crossing "Grade crossing") in [Bethpage](/wiki/Bethpage,_New_York "Bethpage, New York")

### Diesel and dual-mode fleets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Diesel and dual-mode fleets")]

The LIRR also uses 134 [C3](/wiki/C3_(railcar) "C3 (railcar)") [bilevel coaches](/wiki/Bilevel_rail_car "Bilevel rail car") powered by 24 [DE30AC](/wiki/EMD_DE30AC_and_DM30AC "EMD DE30AC and DM30AC") [diesel-electric locomotives](/wiki/Diesel-electric_locomotive "Diesel-electric locomotive") and 20 [DM30AC](/wiki/EMD_DE30AC_and_DM30AC "EMD DE30AC and DM30AC") [dual-mode locomotives](/wiki/Dual-mode_locomotive "Dual-mode locomotive"). They are used mostly on non-electrified branches, including the Port Jefferson, Oyster Bay, Montauk, Central, and Greenport Branches. There are also 23 [MP15AC](/wiki/MP15AC "MP15AC") locomotives in use as work trains and yard switchers.[[153]](#cite_note-153)

## Named trains

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Named trains")]

For most of its history LIRR has served commuters, but it had many named trains, some with all-first class seating, parlor cars, and full bar service. Few of them lasted past World War II, but some names were revived during the 1950s and 1960s, as the railroad expanded its east end parlor car service with luxury coaches and Pullman cars from railroads that were discontinuing their passenger trains.

### Current

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Current")]

* ***[Cannonball](/wiki/Cannonball_(LIRR_train) "Cannonball (LIRR train)")*** – a Friday-only 12-car train to Montauk running May through October, with two all-reserved parlor cars with full bar service. Since May 24, 2013, it has originated at Penn Station with a Sunday evening return from Montauk; only the westward train stops at Jamaica.[[154]](#cite_note-cannonball-new-154) The name is a nod to the *Cannon Ball*, the all-year train to Montauk that had operated since 1899. It carried parlor cars and standard-fare coaches and ran weekday afternoons from Long Island City, then from Penn Station until 1951, when [DD1](/wiki/PRR_DD1 "PRR DD1") operation – and changing engines at Jamaica – ceased.[[155]](#cite_note-trainsarefun.com-155)

### Former

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Former")]

* ***Fisherman's Special*** (1932–1950s) – operated from Long Island City to Canoe Place Station and Montauk via Jamaica, April through October, terminating at Canoe Place in April, extended to Montauk in May. Served Long Island's fishing trade.[[156]](#cite_note-156)
* ***Peconic Bay Express / Shinnecock Bay Express*** (1926–1950) – operated from Long Island City to Greenport and Montauk; Saturday only, express to Greenport and Montauk. Discontinued during World War II though revived for a few seasons afterwards.[[155]](#cite_note-trainsarefun.com-155)
* ***Shelter Island Express*** (1901–1903; 1923–1942) – operated from Long Island City to Greenport; Friday-only summer express that connected to Shelter Island ferries.
* ***Sunrise Special*** (1922–1942) – ran during the summer, from NY Penn to Montauk on Fridays and thence westbound on Mondays. In summer 1926, it ran daily. All parlor car (no coaches) from 1932 to 1937.[[133]](#cite_note-arrts-arrchives.com-133)[[134]](#cite_note-arrts-arrchives1927-134)[[157]](#cite_note-157)[[158]](#cite_note-158)[[159]](#cite_note-159)
* ***Grand Central Direct*** (2023) – operated as a temporary shuttle service between Jamaica and Grand Central Madison, providing service to and from the new terminal.[[160]](#cite_note-:8-160) Operations began on January 25, 2023 – the terminal's opening date – and ceased upon the commencement of regular service the following month.[[160]](#cite_note-:8-160)

## Freight service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Freight service")]

Main article: [Rail freight transportation in New York City and Long Island](/wiki/Rail_freight_transportation_in_New_York_City_and_Long_Island "Rail freight transportation in New York City and Long Island")

For more details on the franchisee that runs freight service for the LIRR, see [New York and Atlantic Railway](/wiki/New_York_and_Atlantic_Railway "New York and Atlantic Railway").

[![A New York and Atlantic freight train at Jamaica station.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/New_York_and_Atlantic_Train_at_Jamaica_LIRR_station_1.jpg/250px-New_York_and_Atlantic_Train_at_Jamaica_LIRR_station_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_York_and_Atlantic_Train_at_Jamaica_LIRR_station_1.jpg)

A [NYAR](/wiki/NYAR "NYAR") freight train at Jamaica station

The LIRR and other railroads that became part of the system have always had freight service, though this has diminished. The process of shedding freight service accelerated with the acquisition of the railroad by New York State.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the 21st century, there has been some appreciation of the need for better [railroad freight service in New York City and on Long Island](/wiki/Rail_freight_transportation_in_New_York_City_and_Long_Island "Rail freight transportation in New York City and Long Island").[[161]](#cite_note-:6-161) Both areas are primarily served by trucking for freight haulage – an irony in a region with the most extensive rail transit service in the Americas, as well as the worst traffic conditions.[[162]](#cite_note-162)

Proposals for a [Cross-Harbor Rail Tunnel](/wiki/Cross-Harbor_Rail_Tunnel "Cross-Harbor Rail Tunnel") for freight have existed for years to alleviate these issues, and, in recent years, there have been many new pushes for its construction by officials.[[161]](#cite_note-:6-161)[[163]](#cite_note-:7-163) Financial issues, as well as bureaucracy, remain major hurdles in constructing it.[[163]](#cite_note-:7-163)
In May 1997, freight service was franchised on a 20-year term to the [New York and Atlantic Railway](/wiki/New_York_and_Atlantic_Railway "New York and Atlantic Railway") (NYAR), a short line railroad owned by the [Anacostia and Pacific Company](/wiki/Anacostia_and_Pacific_Company "Anacostia and Pacific Company").[[164]](#cite_note-164)

It has its own equipment and crews, but uses the rail facilities of the LIRR. To the east, freight service operates to the end of the West Hempstead Branch, to Huntington on the Port Jefferson Branch, to Bridgehampton on the Montauk Branch, and to Riverhead on the Main Line. On the western end it provides service on the surviving freight-only tracks of the LIRR: the Bay Ridge and Bushwick branches; the "Lower Montauk" between Jamaica and Long Island City; and to an interchange connection at [Fresh Pond Junction](/wiki/Fresh_Pond_Junction "Fresh Pond Junction") in Queens with the [CSX](/wiki/CSX "CSX"), [Canadian Pacific Kansas City](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Kansas_City "Canadian Pacific Kansas City"), and [Providence and Worcester](/wiki/Providence_and_Worcester "Providence and Worcester") railroads.[[165]](#cite_note-165)

### Freight branches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Freight branches")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/SotyBrooklynRailway0918.jpg/250px-SotyBrooklynRailway0918.jpg)](/wiki/File:SotyBrooklynRailway0918.jpg)

The freight-only [Bay Ridge Branch](/wiki/Bay_Ridge_Branch "Bay Ridge Branch") through Brooklyn

Some non-electrified lines are used only for freight:

* The [Garden City–Mitchel Field Secondary](/wiki/Garden_City%E2%80%93Mitchel_Field_Secondary "Garden City–Mitchel Field Secondary") is a short remnant of the Central Branch that splits from the Hempstead Branch at Garden City, running to [Uniondale](/wiki/Uniondale,_New_York "Uniondale, New York") near [Hofstra University](/wiki/Hofstra_University "Hofstra University") and Nassau Veterans Memorial Coliseum. This branch does not host any NYAR service. This branch was used by the Ringling Bros. Circus to transport animals, staff and equipment to the Nassau Coliseum until their final shows there in May 2017.[[92]](#cite_note-youtube.com-92)
* The [Bushwick Branch](/wiki/Bushwick_Branch "Bushwick Branch") runs west from the Montauk Branch at [Maspeth](/wiki/Maspeth,_Queens "Maspeth, Queens") to [Bushwick Terminal](/wiki/Bushwick_Terminal "Bushwick Terminal"). This was a passenger branch until 1924.[[166]](#cite_note-Cross_Harbor_EIS_2004_p._128-IA239-166): 128
* The [Bay Ridge Branch](/wiki/Bay_Ridge_Branch "Bay Ridge Branch") runs south and west from the Montauk Branch at [Fresh Pond](/wiki/Fresh_Pond,_Queens "Fresh Pond, Queens") to [Bay Ridge](/wiki/Bay_Ridge,_Brooklyn "Bay Ridge, Brooklyn"). At Fresh Pond, it meets CSX's [Fremont Secondary](/wiki/Fremont_Secondary "Fremont Secondary"), which goes over the [Hell Gate Bridge](/wiki/Hell_Gate_Bridge "Hell Gate Bridge") towards [Upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York") and [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England"). At its southern end it interchanges with the [New York New Jersey Rail](/wiki/New_York_New_Jersey_Rail "New York New Jersey Rail") cross-harbor rail-barge service to New Jersey. This branch had a passenger service until 1924.[[167]](#cite_note-NYCDCP-BklynOpenCut-167) The entire line was [electrified](/wiki/Railway_electrification "Railway electrification") with overhead wire in 1927,[[168]](#cite_note-PRRC_1927-168) and the overhead wires were dismantled in 1969.[[166]](#cite_note-Cross_Harbor_EIS_2004_p._128-IA239-166): 10

## Planned service expansions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Planned service expansions")]

### Electrification projects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Electrification projects")]

[![As part of the MTA's 2020–2024 Capital Program, the Central Branch, shown here, will be electrified.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/DE30_408_West_Babylon_DSC_0384.JPG/250px-DE30_408_West_Babylon_DSC_0384.JPG)](/wiki/File:DE30_408_West_Babylon_DSC_0384.JPG)

A diesel train running along the Central Branch in 2012

As part of the 2020–2024 MTA Capital Program, the MTA proposed electrifying the LIRR's [Central Branch](/wiki/Central_Branch_(Long_Island_Rail_Road) "Central Branch (Long Island Rail Road)"), which would allow for enhanced service options and capacity, and to mitigate service disruptions, should one arise. Although funding was initially allocated through the 2020–2024 MTA Capital Program, the project was ultimately put on hold.[[70]](#cite_note-:2-70)

There have also been many pushes by residents and politicians over the past several decades – most recently by former New York State Senator [Jim Gaughran](/wiki/Jim_Gaughran "Jim Gaughran") – to electrify the remainder of the Port Jefferson Branch between the [Huntington](/wiki/Huntington_station_(LIRR) "Huntington station (LIRR)") and [Port Jefferson](/wiki/Port_Jefferson_station_(LIRR) "Port Jefferson station (LIRR)") stations, in addition to the remainder of the Oyster Bay Branch between the [East Williston](/wiki/East_Williston_station "East Williston station") and [Oyster Bay](/wiki/Oyster_Bay_station "Oyster Bay station"), to enhance service in the served areas and to upgrade service capacities along the lines; electrifying these lines could lead to more frequent direct service to and from Manhattan, as diesel trains are not allowed in Penn Station and dual-mode trains exceed the height clearance for the [63rd Street Tunnel](/wiki/63rd_Street_Tunnel "63rd Street Tunnel") into [Grand Central Madison](/wiki/Grand_Central_Madison "Grand Central Madison").[[169]](#cite_note-169)[[170]](#cite_note-170)[[171]](#cite_note-171)
