The Long Island Rail Road Police Department, founded in 1868,[[172]](#cite_note-:9-172) was absorbed along with the [Metro-North Railroad](/wiki/Metro-North_Railroad "Metro-North Railroad") Police Department to form the [Metropolitan Transportation Authority Police Department (MTA Police)](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority_Police_Department "Metropolitan Transportation Authority Police Department") in 1998.[[172]](#cite_note-:9-172)

## Criticism and controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Criticism and controversy")]

### Passenger issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Passenger issues")]

The LIRR has a long history of tense relations with its passengers.[[173]](#cite_note-173) Daily commuters have long had complaints about the LIRR's service. According to a 1999 article in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times"),* the LIRR's service woes were long considered part of the "unholy trinity of life on Long Island," along with the [Long Island Lighting Company](/wiki/Long_Island_Lighting_Company "Long Island Lighting Company")'s high rates and the [Long Island Expressway](/wiki/Long_Island_Expressway "Long Island Expressway")'s traffic snarls.[[174]](#cite_note-174) Various commuter advocacy groups have been formed to try to represent those interests, in addition to the state mandated LIRR Commuters Council.[[175]](#cite_note-175)

The LIRR has been criticized for not providing additional service to the [East End of Long Island](/wiki/East_End_(Long_Island) "East End (Long Island)") as the twin forks continue to grow in popularity as a year-round tourist and residential destination. Demand is evidenced by flourishing for-profit bus services such as the [Hampton Jitney](/wiki/Hampton_Jitney "Hampton Jitney") and the [Hampton Luxury Liner](/wiki/Hampton_Luxury_Liner "Hampton Luxury Liner") and the early formative stages of a new East End Transportation Authority.[[176]](#cite_note-176) Local politicians have joined the public outcry for the LIRR to either improve the frequency of east end services, or turn the operation over to a local transportation authority.

Critics claim that the [on-time performance](/wiki/On-time_performance "On-time performance") (OTP) calculated by the LIRR is manipulated to be artificially high. Because the LIRR does not release any raw timing data nor does it have independent (non-MTA) audits it is impossible to verify this claim, or the accuracy of the current On Time Performance measurement. The percentage measure is used by many other US passenger railroads but the criticism over accuracy is specific to the LIRR. As defined by the LIRR, a train is "on time" if it arrives at a station within 5 minutes and 59 seconds of the scheduled time.[[177]](#cite_note-177) The criterion was 4 minutes and 59 seconds until the LIRR changed it because of a bug in their computer systems.[[178]](#cite_note-:10-178)

Critics[[179]](#cite_note-179) believe the OTP measure does not reflect what commuters experience on a daily basis. The LIRR publishes the current OTP in a monthly booklet called TrainTalk.[[180]](#cite_note-180) TrainTalk was previously known as "Keeping Track."[[181]](#cite_note-181) A more accurate way to measure delays and OTP has been proposed.[[178]](#cite_note-:10-178) Called the "Passenger Hours Delayed" index, it can measure the total person-hours of a specific delay. This would be useful in comparing the performance of specific days or incidents, day-to-day (or week-to-week) periods, but has not been adopted.

Ridership has increased from 81 million passengers in 2011 to 89.3 million passengers in 2016, which is the railroad's highest ridership since 1949. The all-time highest ridership was in 1929, when 119 million passengers rode 1.89 billion passenger miles.[[182]](#cite_note-182) This increase in ridership has been attributed to the increased usage of the LIRR by millennials, and the increase of reverse-peak travel.[[183]](#cite_note-183)

### Pension and disability fraud scandal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Pension and disability fraud scandal")]

A *[New York Times](/wiki/New_York_Times "New York Times")* investigation in 2008 showed that 25% of LIRR employees who had retired since 2000 filed for disability payments from the federal Railroad Retirement Board and 97% of them were approved to receive disability pension. The total collected was more than $250,000,000 over eight years.[[184]](#cite_note-nyt-184) As a result, Railroad Retirement agents from Chicago inspected the [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") office of the [Railroad Retirement Board](/wiki/Railroad_Retirement_Board "Railroad Retirement Board") on September 23, 2008. New York [Governor](/wiki/List_of_Governors_of_New_York "List of Governors of New York") [David Paterson](/wiki/David_Paterson "David Paterson") issued a statement calling for [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") to conduct a full review of the board's mission and daily activities. Officials at the board's headquarters responded to the investigation stating that all occupational disability annuities were issued in accordance with applicable laws.[[184]](#cite_note-nyt-184)

On November 17, 2008, a former LIRR pension manager was arrested and charged with official misconduct for performing outside work without permission. However, these charges were all dismissed for "no merit" by Supreme Court Judge Kase on December 11, 2009, on the grounds that the prosecution had misled the grand jury in the indictment.[[185]](#cite_note-185)

A report produced in September 2009 by the [Government Accountability Office](/wiki/Government_Accountability_Office "Government Accountability Office") stated that the rate at which retirees were rewarded disability claims was above the norm for the industry in general and indicated "troubling" practices that may indicate fraud, such as the use of a very small group of physicians in making diagnoses.[[186]](#cite_note-186)

Another series of arrests on October 27, 2011, included two doctors and a former union official.[[187]](#cite_note-187)[[188]](#cite_note-188)

According to court documents, from 1998 through 2011, 79% of LIRR retirees obtained federal disability when they retired. On August 6, 2013, a doctor and two consultants were found guilty in connection with the accusations and sentenced to prison.[[189]](#cite_note-189)[[190]](#cite_note-190)[[191]](#cite_note-191)

### Overtime fraud scandals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Overtime fraud scandals")]

In 2018, LIRR foreman Raymond Murphy was discovered at or near his home on 10 separate occasions whilst claiming overtime pay. Murphy earned $405,021 in 2017, of which $295,490 was overtime. According to reports, he was allowed to retire with a full public pension before being reprimanded or punished.[[192]](#cite_note-192)

In 2021, LIRR employee and track inspector Thomas Caputo and co-conspirators John Nugent and Joseph Balestra were federally convicted for large-scale overtime fraud.[[193]](#cite_note-193) Caputo was paid approximately $461,000 in 2018, of which $344,000 was supposed overtime. He claimed to have worked 3,864 overtime hours, an average of more than 10 hours of overtime for all 365 days the year. Phone, bank, email, and other records revealed many of these hours were fraudulent: Caputo was clocked in during vacation and while attending outside social events such as a bowling league.

## Accidents and incidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_Rail_Road&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Accidents and incidents")]

* On August 26, 1893, two trains collided in [Maspeth, Queens](/wiki/Maspeth,_Queens "Maspeth, Queens"), killing 16 people and injuring over 40.[[194]](#cite_note-194)
* On August 13, 1926, the Shelter Island Express derailed on a switch in [Calverton](/wiki/Calverton,_New_York "Calverton, New York"), [Suffolk County](/wiki/Suffolk_County,_New_York "Suffolk County, New York"), and crashed into the Golden Pickle Works factory. The crash claimed six lives and injured 15.[[195]](#cite_note-195)
* On August 3, 1946, a head-on train crash occurred at the [Port Washington station](/wiki/Port_Washington_station "Port Washington station") in [Port Washington](/wiki/Port_Washington,_New_York "Port Washington, New York"). The collision resulted in two deaths and 27 injuries.[[196]](#cite_note-196)
* On [February 17, 1950](/wiki/Rockville_Centre_train_crash "Rockville Centre train crash"), two trains collided head-on after an engineer on train 192 ignored an approach signal and the following red signals at [Rockville Centre station](/wiki/Rockville_Centre_station "Rockville Centre station"), leaving 32 dead and more than 100 injured. At the time, it was the worst rail disaster in LIRR history.[[197]](#cite_note-197)
* On [November 22, 1950](/wiki/Kew_Gardens_train_crash "Kew Gardens train crash"), two trains collided after one of the trains passed a red signal in Kew Gardens, killing 78 and injuring 363 – the LIRR's worst rail disaster to ever occur.[[198]](#cite_note-198)
* On March 14, 1982, a train hit a van at a level crossing on [Herricks Road](/wiki/County_Route_8_(Nassau_County,_New_York) "County Route 8 (Nassau County, New York)") in Mineola after the driver of the van went around the gate. Nine people were killed and one was injured.[[199]](#cite_note-199)
* On [December 7, 1993](/wiki/1993_Long_Island_Rail_Road_shooting "1993 Long Island Rail Road shooting"), a [mass shooting](/wiki/Mass_shooting "Mass shooting") occurred onboard a train at [Merillon Avenue station](/wiki/Merillon_Avenue_station "Merillon Avenue station") in [Garden City Park](/wiki/Garden_City_Park,_New_York "Garden City Park, New York"). Six people were killed and nineteen others were wounded.[[200]](#cite_note-200)
* On October 23, 2000, the lead locomotive ([DM30AC](/wiki/EMD_DE30AC_and_DM30AC "EMD DE30AC and DM30AC") #503) of a dual-mode commuter train caught fire west of [Huntington station](/wiki/Huntington_station_(LIRR) "Huntington station (LIRR)").[[201]](#cite_note-UN-newsday-1024-201)[[202]](#cite_note-UN-newsday-1025-202) The fire was blamed on a defective shoe beam that caused a 750-volt [short circuit](/wiki/Short_circuit "Short circuit") with the locomotive's third rail contact shoe.[[203]](#cite_note-UN-newsday-1026-203)[[204]](#cite_note-UN-NYT-0107-204) The train was evacuated and nobody was injured, though locomotive #503 was irreparably damaged. This incident was the most severe out of several electrical fires involving the then-new DM30AC locomotives, which prompted a full-scale investigation into their reliability and safety.[[203]](#cite_note-UN-newsday-1026-203)[[204]](#cite_note-UN-NYT-0107-204) The entire fleet—two locomotives at a time—would be sent to [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors") for repairs beginning in fall 2001.[[205]](#cite_note-UN-newsday-0621-205)
* On May 17, 2011, a commuter train in Deer Park obliterated a baked goods truck that attempted to drive around the crossing gate. The truck driver was killed and two train passengers were injured.[[206]](#cite_note-206)
* On October 8, 2016, a commuter LIRR train side-swiped a maintenance train east of [New Hyde Park station](/wiki/New_Hyde_Park_station "New Hyde Park station"). The commuter train cars suffered damage and 33 passengers were injured – four of them seriously.[[207]](#cite_note-207)
* On [January 4, 2017](/wiki/2017_Brooklyn_train_crash "2017 Brooklyn train crash"), a Long Island Rail Road commuter train derailed at [Atlantic Terminal](/wiki/Atlantic_Terminal "Atlantic Terminal") in Brooklyn. At least 103 people were injured.[[208]](#cite_note-newsday20170104-208)
* On February 26, 2019, two separate Long Island Rail Road trains hit a pickup truck at the School Street railroad crossing in Westbury on the LIRR Main Line, causing the driver and two passengers to be ejected from the vehicle resulting in their deaths, numerous injuries, and damage to the nearby LIRR station platform.[[209]](#cite_note-209)
* On May 25, 2019, a commuter train sideswiped a [non-revenue train](/wiki/Dead_mileage "Dead mileage") at the siding east of [Speonk station](/wiki/Speonk_station "Speonk station") and derailed. The non-revenue train was 14 cars long, whereas the siding could only fit 13 cars.[[210]](#cite_note-SK-newsday-1030-210) Nobody was injured, though it took two days to restore normal service,[[211]](#cite_note-SK-PIX-0527-211) and the derailed train's front locomotive (DM30AC #511) was damaged beyond repair. The incident was blamed on a track circuit failure;[[210]](#cite_note-SK-newsday-1030-210) an MTA worker, who resigned following the accident, was later charged and indicted for falsifying an inspection report of the would-be point of failure.[[212]](#cite_note-SK-feds-212)[[213]](#cite_note-SK-patch-213)
* On August 3, 2023, a Long Island Rail Road commuter train derailed east of Jamaica station; 13 people were injured.[[214]](#cite_note-ABC7_New_York_2023_a245-214)
* On April 17, 2025, a Long Island Rail Road commuter train collided with a vehicle west of Pinelawn station. The driver of the car was killed and the first car of the train was damaged.[[215]](#cite_note-215)
